sentences.text: Course.
Hey there, Mark.
Hey there.
How are you all?
I'm doing good.
How are you?
Good.
Thank you very much.
Awesome.
Awesome.
How's the week going so far?
You know, it's, it's, it's been a week already, but today is the end of my week.
So that's not necessarily a bad thing for me.
Yeah, you, you mentioned you're taking some time off or going on vacation or what's, what's going on?
Yeah, I'm off for the next 2.5 weeks headed to Asia.
So um oh,
look forward to it.
Yeah.
Where, where in Asia are you going?
Uh starting in South Korea, then I'm gonna explore three islands around the Philippines, then Taiwan and ending in Tokyo for 34 days, something like that.
That's incredible.
Is, is this a trip you've been planning for a while?
You know, um my partner and I, we have friends that recently got married and one of them is Filipino and said, hey, want to want to introduce my new husband to my family.
So we're going to the Philippines.
Do you all want to go with us?
And we decided this on a whim.
I over happy hour in January and here we go.
So,
yes.
No.
In a way we've been talking about hitting up this part of Asia for a while because we've done Hong Kong.
We've done Vietnam, we've done Thailand.

Um but we've been talking about it for a while, but the, the decision was kind of a impromptu quick decision, but that's sometimes how it has to happen, you know, like we always talk about like wanting to travel certain places and then sometimes it just takes initiative to have someone that, you know, that might be over there and then she got to buy a plane ticket and do it, you know.
Correct.
Correct.
So, well, anyways, it's really good to meet you.
We were excited to connect with you.
We've been looking forward to not only um learning a little bit more about you and Simpson today, but um kind of walking you through the platform.
Um I have my colleague and my director Justin on the call with us today who's gonna be um showing us the platform.
But um if you don't mind, I'd love to kind of ask you a couple of questions to um learn a little bit more about you guys and then um we can jump into things.
Sound like a good plan.
Let's do it perfect.

Well, I guess I'm kind of curious to know a little bit more about your current market survey process, maybe tools that are used internally to enable that and kind of where you're at and maybe where you as a company envision yourself going to maybe optimize things.
I mean, we rely on a few different data points.
Um uh Besides the weekly call around, that's kind of an Excel driven document that then ends up getting fed into our revenue management software.
Um We're still on LRO so we are gonna be making a change from that sometime soon.
But as of right now, um you know, a weekly update for that, the rest of it, then we rely on Axio metrics or now real pages market analytics.
Um We do subscribe to real pages benchmarking tool, which they don't tell us what communities they benchmark us against.
It's like communities in a, you know, similar size, similar class of asset with an X radius of the community.
Uh We do benchmark ourselves against Green Street.
So we do look at a lot of, you know, a lot of that is just forecasts and then actuals at Green Street reports on our reap peers.
Um And then every now and then we'll look at co stars reports, but that report is
neither here nor there.
Um And then we also subscribe to a DS um MRI software on um on that.
So a few different tools and I guess depending on the case that I'm trying to build which report I pull.
Um but that's kind of where it's at.
So it's a bit all over the place.
And our biggest concern right now is, of course, with all the litigation against multifamily operators.
When it comes to pricing revenue management software, there's so many of our competitors that aren't giving, uh, aren't releasing market survey information.
Um So there's just a lot of time that's being spent scouring through websites.
Um, trying to figure out is that one apartment that's discounted, is it all of them, whatever the case is?
Um And it makes it a little bit difficult to operate because at the end of the day, I mean, quite frankly, we want our competitors to do good so we can do good.
We don't want to be the cheapest, we don't want to be the most expensive.
We just want accurate data.
Totally.
Totally.
That's very well said, man, you were unpacking a lot that we were already kind of curious about.
So I, yeah, I appreciate you sharing.

Um going back to sort of the public versus private, you know, litigation concerns is, would you say that that's sort of a, you know, an issue within Simpson, like in a direction you guys are wanting to take, you know, away from that sort of contingencies.
I mean, here's the thing.
We,
yes and no.
Um I it's impacting some of our decisions, but at the end of the day, our concern has, has uh was routed in something that propped up prior to this litigation popping up.
We've always just wanted objective true market survey data and understanding what's going on.
Um for us to be able to speak to our investors, um speak to the value of the asset, um accurately forecast and project a lot of our peers um spend a lot of time reporting on what's already happened.
Um I'm already projecting Q three at this point.
Um And a lot of that, you know how my Q three is gonna look is based off of what I'm leasing apartments for today.
So um
I can't really give you an answer on that.
Yes, it's impacted some of our operational decisions, but at the same point, it's something we've been looking at for quite some time.
No, that's good.
That's good.
I know it's kind of a hard question to answer sometimes.
I guess that was kind of lead me to my next question.
You know, what are you looking for out of, you know, data from the next platform or, you know, it sounds like you got a lot of different solutions and different processes in place right now.
Like what is the most ideal scenario look like for you guys?
Is it sort of like one consolidated source of data that is up to date and accurate and true or um I'm sorry if you've already kind of said these things.

I mean, ideally, yes, one consolidated source that is accurate um where I take the burden off my site team so they can manage their assets you know, of course, as we look at staffing efficiencies, rolling out A I this, that the other can we cut back on staff?
And we're like, ah, I need to reverse course on that and impacts kind of our customer reputation scores.

But as customers get more involved and more demanding, I would rather be able to free up my site team from looking through four plus different websites twice a week and get the data from somewhere else.
Yeah, exactly.
Exactly.
Um And you guys are 100% fee managed.
Is that correct?
No, we are an owner operator.
Um So we
uh we fee manage for one client, but we have a joint venture with that client.
So we have partial ownership and what we fee manage for them.
They're just listed as the owner and we're shareholders in the fund that we fee manage for.
Sure.
Sure, sure, sure.
What is sort of the total portfolio size look like right now at Simpson.
Uh we're up to 76 assets, 24,000 Simon units under management.
Awesome.
Ok.
Um Justin, do you have any questions top of mind right now?
Yeah, Mark, I was gonna say you mentioned you're kind of eventually gonna move away from LRO is that you're gonna take away pricing or take pricing in house or move it over to a IRM.
Um We're looking at a few different options.
We already manage LRO in house.
So by that of course, we have our own revenue managers that do all the input for LRO we don't rely on real page.
Um We don't pay real page for any sort of revenue management services other than access to the software.
You guys aren't meeting, meeting up with their advisory services once a week or anything like that.
Correct.
We have that in house.
Nice.
And I was gonna ask you, you, you mentioned um kind of depending on the data you're trying to pull together.
You, you may get us some different sources, I guess for you as far as communicating with or getting the data.
Do you use it to communicate with on site?
Team members used to communicate with revenue?
I guess what's kind of your normal flow in terms of the data?
I mean, it goes both ways um in, in my role, um It's both communicating to, you know, our SVP as well as our president when there's a concern.
Um The one asset manager I'm involved with that we fee manage for.
Um And then also my site teams um making sure that they're understanding how they're pricing their apartment homes, what, what's going on in their area um and all that fun stuff.
So it flows in both directions.
Nice makes sense.
Um I guess last thing do, do you know much about apartment IQ?
Um Not much so
cool.
Well, I'll let you know, I, I think it's a pretty easy conversation.
Um I always say that there's four things that make our product really unique.
Um The first thing being is that we're fully automated.
So there's zero manual data collection.
Whats the weather uh whatsoever, whether it be from us or from your on site teams.
So we can completely eliminate the need of them to them to go out there and call comps or, or surf websites themselves.
Um The second thing and I think maybe the most important thing is that we actually update our data every single day.
So as you're aware, you know, properties out there, they're on these rev tools, they're changing rights regularly.
So our goal is that any time your team wants to understand the market, you could feel confident jumping into our platform and seeing what's going on today.
Um What else is great is we can also track any property out there as long as they have a website and use a property management software.
They do not have to be apartment IQ customers whatsoever.
So we can truly track the entire market, which is great and uh just for transparency.
So the way that we collect our data is our tool actually goes out to these property websites, collects the data every single night.
And more specifically, we actually go to the online leasing section of the property website, which is being powered by that property management software.
So that's why in order for us to track a comp, they do have to have a website and use a property management software.
Um, last thing then too, we kind of touched on it but, uh, everything inside of apartment IQ is 100% publicly available data.
So, you know, over the last year, uh, I would say that we've dealt more with the legal side of this business than we ever thought we would.
Um, we work with some of the, the biggest companies in the industry, um some of the most litigious companies, but we've uh we've been put through the ringer.
We always come out very clean.

Um We're, we're great partners for those that are concerned about the sharing of private data just because again, we are literally reflecting back to you what is publicly available and what a renter can see when they visit your site or visit a, a competitor's website.
Understood so cool

and, and for what it's worth to in regards to um to like to like LRO or another rev tool, um probably 75 80% of our current customers use IQ in conjunction with one of those uh revenue management tools.
So we're not trying to replace it whatsoever.
I think we're, we're, we're actually a really nice compliment to it.
And you even mentioned like uh with real pages benchmarking tool about how they just give you the aggregated data.
You don't necessarily see which um competitor, you know, they're comparing you against.
Well, with us, we get extremely granular so we break the data down at the property level, the floor plan level and all the way down to the unit level.
So you can see where a lot of those trends or anomalies might be coming from.
Perfect.
Yeah, but I think that's
what makes it difficult like um we bonus our community managers based off of lease trade out.

And in a lot of cases when I'm looking at Sky House, Austin, for example, on Rainy Street, downtown Austin, the sub market isn't doing well, but the Rainy Street sub market is even more impacted with the seven towers that are currently under construction.
When comparing us, they're benchmarking us against six other assets.
Well, there's only two other multifamily assets on Rainy Street now.
So I know they're pulling in something.
Um you know, either from east, the east side of 35 or something uh further in downtown Austin.
And then all of a sudden, I'm missing my trade outs and my community manager is like, I'm doing the best that I can.
This is gonna impact my bonus and, and that's when I start having to spend way more time digging for information um than I should have.
And I think sometimes it leads to some decisions that are made based off of the fact that I've been in the business for 20 years.
And my gut tells me to do this instead of having the data to kind of back up our, our our thought
process.
Yeah, absolutely.
That makes sense.
And, and that, actually, it's actually a good kind of segue too.
How, how is your team identifying comps right now?
Is it just kind of knowing your markets or do you have a process in place or off of the performers?
I guess, what's the, the
process conly, uh, monitor, you know, any sort of new deliveries or whatever the case is?
We tend not to weigh against those comps too heavily when they're in lease up just because of how aggressive they can be, especially if they're merchant builder,
you know, fill up fast and sell.

Um with that, all of our assets are on a 18 to 24 month kind of rotation where the revenue manager, the regional, the community manager and the marketing manager all meet and tour the sub market and basically pick our comps and for the next 18 to 24 months, those are the communities that we comp against.
So, um you know, it, it's based off of kind of feet in the street touring the assets like products.
Who are we trading traffic with?
Um, you know, that's one of the reasons that we rely on co stars reporting.
We wanna see whoever's looking us up on apartments.com, what other community are they looking up in the area?
Um So a few different sources to come up with our comps.
Nice.
Now that, that makes perfect sense and, and the reason I was asking that is because whenever we set these property cards up, your team's in complete control of them.
So typically, what happens is the customer would say, hey, you know, these are the conf be tracking, set up the data that way.
Um However, we do also have a comp matching algorithm where we can make some suggestions too based off of a handful of different variables.
Um But nonetheless, I mean, your team is always in complete control of the comps.
If you ever want to add a property to your comp set, you can do it right in the app, if you ever want to remove a property, same thing.
So you can truly track whoever is relevant to you.

And even again, since this is an automated solution, maybe it makes sense to track, you know, one or two extra comps, you normally would just because it's pressing a button rather than having to kind of surf the website or pick up.
Understood.
Yeah.
But yeah, the way it works is like I said, each property would get its own card and then the data is based off of that property specific concept.
Um I will also mention too that we offer unlimited users.
We do not charge per seat.
So a lot of our customers do give access to the on site teams all the way up a little bit of a different value point for each uh for, for each role and, and for what it's worth too.
We do a nice job of, of, of training every single user.
Um So we do group sessions, one on one sessions, ongoing training sessions.
So whoever your team would wanna have access to the platform, we do a nice job of making sure that they can maximize their experience in the tool.
But yeah, you click in into a property and then it'll take you into the data.
So once you're in here at the very top, this will be your main navigation.
So each property does have four reports associated with it.
Uh The first report which we're on here is the market survey.
And then if you select the data drop down, you'll see three additional reports as well.
But we'll start with the market survey since we're on it.
Um I think it's very, you know, very representative of your standard market survey.
Uh very simple, straightforward.
Uh We simply list all of your competitors across the top and then we also include your property, which would be highlighted here in the indigo.
And then the idea here is just to be able to quickly compare your property's performance versus the competition.
So you'll notice there's two different sections here.
Um What's nice is that all of the metrics in both of these sections are sort from low to high and high to low.
So you can look and, and, and understand kind of like I said, how you're performing versus the competition.
So first section here, this is gonna be your property summary.
So a lot of high level property data, um some of those performance metrics, you know, we'll show you things like advertised occupancy percentage, we show you exposure percentage.
So the percent of units that are available in each property, um we show that over a couple of different time horizons.
Uh We'll show you the number of units that are available in a property right now of those units that are available.
How many are vacant?
You also notice that we provide some transactional data as well.
So the number of leases that each property has over the last 30 days, seven days and the number of leases that each property does average per week.
And lastly, what's great is that we do track every single rent special concession um As long as it's on that property website.
So how are you getting the advertised occupancy?
Exposure makes sense.
You know, the unit count, you can see how many apartments are advertising online.
You can get that from their website and you can figure out the next 6030 days based off of that as well, based off of what day the apartments coming available.
That's great.
Everything makes sense.
With the exception of the advertised occupancy.
Where are you getting that
from?
Yep, great question.
So we take and then this is the reason we call it advertised occupancy and not just occupancy, but we base this metric off the known number of vacant units in the property.
And what I mean by that has to do with some properties advertising practices.
So most common in a lease up, but sometimes a property won't actually show their full gambit of availability.
So, you know, if a property is 31 bedrooms available, they only decide to make 10 available on the property website.
Our system can only account for the 10 that are made available on the property website.
So that's why we call that advertised occupancy percentage.
And there's, there's a bit of a variation there, I'm assuming based off of pre leased vacants that are not showing available have been leased, whatever the case is.
Right.
Um And then last question, how are you getting your number of leases?
Yep.
So leases is pretty similar.
So since we're tracking each unit individually, if the unit is available on a property website today and then it's gone tomorrow, we will assume that that unit has been leased.
Got it.
You've answered all my questions.
Cool.
Yeah.

I mean, every data point metric calculation in here.
If you have ever question where they come from, I can pull up the source and show you.
I could say this is exactly where we're getting it from.
This is on the website where it's coming from as opposed to, you know, if you pick up the phone and talk to a leasing agent?
Are they telling you what's available?
They tell you, you know, kind of what's hidden behind the scenes or are they making something up?

So essentially this is what's dictating the market here.

you available as well?
